title: A Theory of Equivalent Expectation Measures for Contingent Claim Returns
abstract: This paper introduces a dynamic change of measure approach for computing analytical solutions of expected future prices (and therefore, expected returns) of contingent claims over a finite horizon. The new approach constructs hybrid probability measures called equivalent expectation measures (EEMs) that provide the physical expectation of the claim's future price before the horizon date, and serve as pricing measures on or after the horizon date. The EEM theory can be used for empirical investigations of both the cross-section and the term structure of returns of contingent claims, such as Treasury bonds, corporate bonds, and financial derivatives.

article.introduction:
The change of measure conceptlies at the heart of asset pricing. Almost half a century ago, the no-arbitrage derivations of the call option price by Black and Scholes (1973) and Merton (1973b) led to the discovery of the standard change of measure approach, which assigns risk-neutral or equivalent martingale probabilities to all future events.1Extending this static approach, which changes the pricing measure only at the current time, we develop adynamicchange of measure approach that changes the pricing measure at any given future horizon dateHbetween the current timetand the claim's expiration dateT. Using the new dynamic change of measure approach, this paper develops a theory for computing expected future prices, and therefore, expected returns of contingent claims over any finite horizonH, and hence significantly broadens the range of applications of the change of measure approach in finance.
The theory developed in this paper can be used to answer the following kinds of questions. What is the expected return of a 10-year Treasury bond over the next two months under theA1(3) affine model of Dai and Singleton (2000)? What is the expected return of a five-year, A-rated corporate bond over the next quarter under the stationary-leverage ratio model of Collin-Dufresne and Goldstein (2001)? What is the expected return of a three-month call option on a stock over the next month under the SVJ model of Pan (2002) or under the CGMY model of Carr et al. (2002)? What is the expected return of a five-year interest rate cap over the next six months, under the QTSM3 interest rate model of Ahn, Dittmar, and Gallant (2002)? While the analytical solutions of the current pricesexistunder all of the above-mentioned models, the analytical solutions of the expected future prices—which are necessary inputs for computing the expected returns—over an arbitrary finite horizonHdo not existin the finance literature.
Estimating expected returns becomes difficult, if not impossible, for most buy-side firms and other buy-and-hold investors, who do not continuously rebalance and hedge their portfolios, without the analytical solutions of expected future prices of the financial securities in their portfolios. This problem is faced not only by sophisticated hedge fund managers who hold complex contingent claims, but also by numerous asset managers at mutual funds, banks, insurance companies, and pension funds who hold the most basic financial securities, such as U.S. Treasury bonds and corporate bonds. In an insightful study, Becker and Ivashina (2015) illustrate this problem in the context of insurance companies, whose asset managers reach for higher (promised) yields and not necessarily higher expected returns. They find that the “yield-centered” holdings of insurance companies are related to the business cycle, being most pronounced during economic expansions. More generally, it is common to use metrics based upon yield, rating, sector/industry, optionality, and default probability to make portfolio-holding decisions by fixed-income asset managers. Since expected returns are not modeledexplicitly, these metrics provide at best only rough approximations, and at worst counterintuitive guidance about the ex-ante expected returns as shown by Becker and Ivashina (2015). This is quite unlike the equity markets, where both academics and portfolio managers spend much effort to obtain the conditional estimates of the expected returns on stocks. Part of this widespread problem in the fixed income markets and the financial derivatives markets is due to the lack of a simple and parsimonious framework that can guide the conditional estimation of finite-horizon expected returns. Our paper fills this gap in the finance theory.

The discrete-time example above uses the money market account as the numeraire and the risk-neutral measureQ${\mathbb {Q}}$as the corresponding equivalent martingale measure (EMM) for valuation on or after the horizon dateH. The theory underpinning this example can be generalized to continuous-time models that use alternative numeraires for the valuation of contingent claims. The mathematical intuition underlying this new theory can be understood as follows.
Given a numeraireGwith the corresponding EMMQ∗${\mathbb {Q}^*}$and the Radon-Nikodým derivative processL∗$L^*$, we show how the expected future prices of contingent claims can be computed using one of two new measures,R∗${\mathbb {R}^*}$andR1∗${{\mathbb {R}}^{*}_1}$, with Radon-Nikodým derivative processesL∗$\mathcal {L}^*$andL1∗$\mathcal {L}_1^*$, respectively. Each of the new measures is pieced together using two different Radon-Nikodým derivative processes, which break from each other at the horizon dateH, so that the measure provides the physical expectation of the claim's time-Hfuture price until before timeHand serves as the pricing (or the equivalent martingale) measure on or after timeH. This simple and novel approach is very useful in providing formulas for the expected future prices of contingent claims, which are of the same general form as the corresponding valuation formulas of these claims, requiring only that the forms of the underlying price processes be similar under the physical measure and the pricing measure.
Unlike the traditional EMMs, discounted prices are martingales under the new measures,R∗${\mathbb {R}^*}$andR1∗${{\mathbb {R}}^{*}_1}$, only from timeHto timeT—not from timetto timeH. Since these new measures provide the physical expectation of the claim's timeHfuture price prior to timeH, we denote these measures asequivalent expectation measures(EEMs). The EMMs of Harrison and Kreps (1979) and Geman, El Karoui, and Rochet (1995) are obtained as special cases of the corresponding EEMs, whent=H$t = H$.
Equations (2), (4), and (4a) provide three distinct but related methods for computing the expected future price of the call option. SectionIgeneralizes the dynamic change of method given by equation (2) to hold with stochastic interest rates and alternative numeraire assets. While the generalized dynamic change of method is mathematically equivalent to thefutureSDF method given by equation (4) and thedynamicchange of numeraire method given by equation (4a), the former method has a significant advantage over the latter two methods for the derivations of the analytical solutions of the expected future prices under virtually all contingent claim models that admit an analytical solution to their current price using an EMM.
Though this paper provides the first formal theory of the dynamic change of measure method, this is not the first paper to propose a change of measure at a future date. Rubinstein's (1984) formula for computing the expected return of a call option under the Black and Scholes (1973) model, and the valuation formulas for a forward-start option by Rubinstein (1991) and Kruse and Nögel (2005), also use a change of measure at a future date. These papers combine two pricing measures or EMMs iteratively—one measure from timeHto timeTfor calculating the valuation at timeH, and another from the current timetto timeHfor calculating the current expectation—which is a technique that works under a limited set of models. In contrast, our paper creates a new class of hybrid measures called EEMs that ensure derivations of analytical solutions to the expected future prices of contingent claims under virtually all models in the finance literature that admit an analytical solution to their current price using existing EMMs.
Given the central role of expected returns in empirical asset pricing and in investment management, the additional flexibility provided by the analytical solutions of the expected future prices of contingent claims over different future horizons can significantly enhance our understanding of the investment opportunity set available in the Treasury bond and corporate bond markets, in virtually all of the financial derivatives in equity, interest rate, foreign exchange, commodity, credit risk, and in other areas of finance. A general econometric implication of the EEM theory is that the analytical solutions of the expected returns over different horizons can allow applications of various empirical estimation methods, such as linear regression, the Markov chain Monte Carlo method, generalized method of moments (see Hansen (1982)), and others, for studying both the cross-section and the term structure of contingent claims returns in different markets.
For example, future research can evaluate the return prediction ability of different structural models for corporate bonds using model-implied3analytical solutions of the expected returns, extending the empirical investigations of Gebhardt, Hvidkjaer, and Swaminathan (2005), Lin, Wang, and Wu (2011), Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019), Chung, Wang, and Wu (2019), and Bali, Subrahmanyam, and Wen (2021). Similarly, future research can evaluate the return prediction ability of different dynamic term structure models (DTSMs) for Treasury bonds using the analytical solutions of the expected returns, extending the empirical investigations of Dai and Singleton (2000,2002), Ahn, Dittmar, and Gallant (2002), Duffee (2002), Bansal and Zhou (2002), Hong and Li (2005), Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008), and Joslin, Singleton, and Zhu (2011). To the best of our knowledge, Adrian, Crump, and Moench (2013) is the only existing study that directly incorporates the bond return information for model estimation using regressions of one-period excess returns. The availability of analytical solutions of the expected bond returns over different horizons can also provide new insights on theterm structureof bond returns. While it may not be possible to study the term structure of corporate bond returns due to paucity of data and liquidity issues over short horizons, the DTSMs can be adapted for Treasury bond return prediction by forcing them to match the term structure of returns instead of the term structure of zero-coupon yields.
In the options area, the availability of analytical solutions of the expected option returns for different horizons may not only advance our knowledge about the term structure of option returns, but also provide additional insight into the debate on whether index options are mispriced. For example, based on high negative index put option expected returns over amonthlyhorizon, Coval and Shumway (2001) and Chambers et al. (2014) argue for mispricing, while Broadie, Chernov, and Johannes (2009) and Baele et al. (2019) argue for no mispricing. Whether using the analytical solutions of the expected option returns over different return horizons of one day, two days, one week, two weeks, and up to one month would add more insight on this empirical debate remains an open question.
Finally, as an empirical application in the risk management area, the framework of this paper can be extended to compute variance, covariance, and other higher-order moments, as well as various tail risk measures routinely used in risk-and-return analysis of fixed income portfolios and equity portfolios with embedded options.
This paper is organized as follows. SectionIdevelops the theory of two general EEMs,R∗${\mathbb {R}^*}$andR1∗${{\mathbb {R}}^{*}_1}$, using the dynamic change of measure method. These measures lead to three numeraire-specific EEMs: (i) theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure from theR∗${\mathbb {R}^*}$class using the money market account as the numeraire; (ii) theR1T${{\mathbb {R}}^{T}_1 }$measure from theR1∗${{\mathbb {R}}^{*}_1}$class using theT-maturity pure discount bond as the numeraire; and (iii) theR1S${{\mathbb {R}}^{S}_1 }$measure from theR1∗${{\mathbb {R}}^{*}_1}$class using any general assetSother than the money market account and theT-maturity pure discount bond as the numeraire. These three numeraire-specific EEMs allow for derivation of analytical solutions to the expected future prices of virtually all contingent claims in finance that are priced using the change of measure method.
SectionIIillustrates the application of theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure using the examples of the Black and Scholes (1973) option pricing model and the affine term structure models (ATSMs) of Dai and Singleton (2000) and Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008). This section also presents newR-transforms based on theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure, which can be used to obtain analytical solutions of the expected future prices of contingent claims that are priced using the transform-based models of Bakshi and Madan (2000), Duffie, Pan, and Singleton (2000), and Chacko and Das (2002) and the CGMY Lévy model of Carr et al. (2002).
SectionIIIillustrates the application of theR1T${{\mathbb {R}}^{T}_1 }$measure using the examples of the Merton (1973b) option pricing model and the structural debt pricing model of Collin-Dufresne and Goldstein (2001). This section also extends the theoretical framework of Breeden and Litzenberger (1978) to obtain theR1T${{\mathbb {R}}^{T}_1 }$density and the physical density of the underlying asset using nonparametric and semiparametric empirical models of expected option returns.

We show that while the valuation of the exchange option does not depend on interest rates under the Margrabe (1978) model, the expected future price of the exchange option does depend on the parameters of the interest rate process.
SectionIVconsiders some theoretical extensions of the EEM theory and provides concluding remarks. Proofs of some of the theorems and propositions are provided in theAppendix. TheInternet Appendixpresents some of the models in more detail.

TheR${\mathbb {R}}$measure can be used to derive the expected future prices of contingent claims under the following models:10the equity option pricing models of Black and Scholes (1973), Cox and Ross (1976), Merton (1976), Hull and White (1987), and Rubinstein (1991); the corporate debt pricing models of Merton (1974), Black and Cox (1976), and Leland and Toft (1996); the term structure models of Dai and Singleton (2000,2002), Ahn, Dittmar, and Gallant (2002), Leippold and Wu (2003), and Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008) for pricing default-free bonds; the credit default swap pricing model of Longstaff, Mithal, and Neis (2005); the VIX futures and the variance swaps models of Dew-Becker et al. (2002), and others, summarized in SectionII.C, among others.
In this section, we present theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure using the examples of the Black and Scholes (1973) option pricing model, the ATSMs of Dai and Singleton (2000) and Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008), and the Fourier transform-based model of Duffie, Pan, and Singleton (2000).
The Black-Scholes call price formula has also been used as part of the solution of option valuation models with more complex stochastic processes and/or terminal boundary conditions. For example, Merton (1976) solves the call option price under a jump diffusion model using a weighted sum of an infinite number of Black-Scholes call prices, and Rubinstein (1991) solves the price of a forward-start call option (FSO) as a constant share of the underlying asset's price, where the constant is determined using the Black-Scholes call price. These frameworks can be extended for computing the analytical solution of the expected future call price under these complex option valuation models by using the Black-Scholes expected future call price given by equations (19) and (C1),as a part of their solutions. We demonstrate this insight in AppendixDby extending Rubinstein's (1991) framework to obtain the expected future price of an FSO using theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure.

We would like to make two important observations in the context of the discussion above. First, an analytical solution ofEt[P(H,T)]${\mathbb {E}}_t[ P(H,T)]$given by equation (20) can generally be derived using theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure for any DTSM that admits an analytical solution for the current price of the zero-coupon bond using theQ${\mathbb {Q}}$measure. Second, it is generally not possible to know the analytical solution ofEt[P(H,T)]${\mathbb {E}}_t[ P(H,T)]$by knowing the analytical solution ofEt[ln(P(H,T))]${\mathbb {E}}_t[ \ln (P(H,T)) ]$, or vice versa, for most term structure models except over the infinitesimal horizonH=t+dt$H = t + dt$.15Thus, even though the estimates of the expected log returns over a finite horizon have been obtained by many researchers (usually using one-month returns), such estimates cannot be used by fixed income investors to compute theexpected returnsof bonds and bond portfolios over a finite horizon, without making additional return distribution assumptions.
To derive the expected returns of default-free bonds, we derive the expected future price of a pure discount bond at any finite horizonH(for allt≤H≤T$t \le H \le T$) using the ATSMs of Dai and Singleton (2000) and Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008). Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008) present a new method to obtain maximal ATSMs that uses infinitesimal maturity yields and their quadratic covariations as the globally identifiable state variables. The new method allows model-independent estimates for the state vector that can be estimated directly from yield curve data. When using three or less state variables, which is typical in empirical research on term structure models, the new approach of Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008) is consistent with the general framework of Dai and Singleton (2000) given below, but with some clear advantages in the estimation and interpretation of the economic state variables.16This framework is also consistent with the unspanned stochastic volatility-based ATSMs of Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2009) with appropriate parameter restrictions on the stochastic processes.
Recall from the previous section that any stochastic process under theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure is the physical stochastic process prior to horizonH, and is the risk-neutral process on or after the horizonH. Thus, the state variable process under theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure in equation (25a) can also be obtained by simple inspection of the state variable process under theP${\mathbb {P}}$measure given by equation (23) and under theQ${\mathbb {Q}}$measure given by equation (24a).
Equation (30) can be used to obtain the expected future price of a pure discount bond under any specific ATSM of Dai and Singleton (2000) and Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Jones (2008). We demonstrate three examples inInternet AppendixSectionII. Specifically,Internet AppendixSectionsII.AandII.Bgive analytical solutions of the expected future price of a pure discount bond under the Vasicek model and the Cox-Ingersoll-Ross (CIR) model, respectively. To our knowledge, these equations provide the first analytical solutions of the expected future price of a pure discount bond under the Vasicek and CIR models in the finance literature. Using a tedious derivation,Internet AppendixSectionII.Cgives the expected future price of the pure discount bond under theA1r(3)$A_{1r}(3)$model of Dai and Singleton (2000).Internet AppendixSectionIIIderives a general solution to the expected future price of a pure discount bond under the maximal quadratic term structure model (QTSM), as well as a specific solution under the QTSM3 model (see Ahn, Dittmar, and Gallant (2002)).
In two path-breaking papers, Bakshi and Madan (2000) and Duffie, Pan, and Singleton (2000) derive new transforms that extend the Fourier transform-based method of Heston (1993) to the valuation of a wide variety of contingent claims. Though Chacko and Das (2002) also independently develop a generalized Fourier transform-based method for the valuation of a variety of interest rate derivatives, their approach can be obtained as a special case of the more general transform-based method of Duffie, Pan, and Singleton (2000). Since the specific transforms in these papers use the risk-neutral measureQ${\mathbb {Q}}$in the valuation, we refer to these transforms asQ-transforms.
This section derives newR-transforms using theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure to obtain the expected future prices of a wide variety of contingent claims that are priced using theQ-transforms of Bakshi and Madan (2000), Duffie, Pan, and Singleton (2000), and Chacko and Das (2002). TheR-transforms nest the correspondingQ-transforms for the special case in whichH=t$H=t$. TheR-transforms can be used to derive analytical solutions of the expected future prices of contingent claims that are priced under the following models: the affine option pricing models of Heston (1993), Bates (1996,2000), Bakshi, Cao, and Chen (1997), Pan (2002), Bakshi and Madan (2000), Duffie, Pan, and Singleton (2000), and Chacko and Das (2002); the Lévy option pricing models of Carr et al. Nawalkha and Zhuo (2022) obtain analytical solutions of the expected future prices of equity options and a variety of interest rate derivatives using some of the models above.

Thus far, we have developed the EEM theory in the context of parametric models assuming thesame formfor the state variable processes under both the physical measure and the given EMM. This assumption does not always hold empirically. For example, the physical process and the risk-neutral process for the asset return cannot have the same form under the cumulative prospect theory model of option returns given by Baele et al. (2019). This section gives additional theoretical results related to the EEM theory that do not require strong parametric assumptions.
To derive these results, we assume that the expected future prices of standard European options with a sufficiently large range of strike prices have been estimated using nonparametric or semiparametric methods (e.g., see Coval and Shumway (2001), Bondarenko (2003,2014), Jones (2006), Driessen and Maenhout (2007), Bakshi, Madan, and Panayotov (2010), Constantinides, Jackwerth, and Savov (2013), Israelov and Kelly (2017), Baele et al. (2019), Büchner and Kelly (2022)). Using these expected future option prices, we show how one can obtainboththeR1T${{\mathbb {R}}^{T}_1 }$density and the physical density of the underlying asset return using an extension of the theoretical framework of Breeden and Litzenberger (1978). Furthermore, we show that theR1T${{\mathbb {R}}^{T}_1 }$density can be used to obtain the expected future prices (and expected returns) of a wider range of complex European contingent claims with terminal payoffs that are arbitrary functions of the underlying asset's future price.
To fix ideas, assume that the Arrow-Debreu price density or the SPD of the underlying asset at current timetis given by the functionft(ST)$f_t(S_T)$, whereST$S_T$is the future price of this asset at timeT. Equation (43) shows that the second derivative of the expected future call price function with respect to the strike price gives the expected FSPD, which equals the discountedR1T${{\mathbb {R}}^{T}_1 }$probability density, similar to how the SPD equals the discounted forward densityQT${\mathbb {Q}^T}$in Breeden and Litzenberger (1978). If the expected FSPD can be econometrically estimated using the expected future prices of standard European call and put options,29then it can be used to obtain the expected future prices (and expected returns) of a wider range of complex European contingent claims with terminal payoffs that are arbitrary functions of the underlying asset's future price as shown in equation (45). This can be useful if a researcher desires consistency between the expected returns of such complex claims and those of standard European options without making strong parametric assumptions about the underlying state variable processes.
A surprisingly simple and overlooked result in the option pricing literature is given by the last equality in equations (43) and (44), according to which the expectedterminalFSPD (i.e.,gt(T,ST)$g_t(T, S_T)$)—defined as the second derivative of the expected terminal call (or put) price with respect to the strike price—equals the physical density of the underlying asset's future price. This arbitrage-based, tautologically exact relationship between the expected terminal FSPD and the physical density is especially relevant in light of recent theoretical and empirical research that investigates the relationship between the SPD and the physical density (see Ross (2015), Martin and Wagner (2019), Jensen, Lando, and Pedersen (2019), Jackwerth and Menner (2020)).30The empirical implications of Theorem3can be explored by future research on nonparametric and semiparametric option return models.
article.IV. Extensions and Conclusion:
Second, consider those claims that may not be alive until timeHin some states of nature. For example, consider a standard American option that can be exercised at any time until the expiration date of the option. While the expected future price of an American option cannot be computed, since the option is not alive at timeHin some states of nature, the expected future price of areinvestment strategythat invests the value of the American option upon exercise in another security—for example, anH-maturity pure discount bond or a money market account—until timeH, can be computed numerically using theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure.
Third, consider those claims that pay out a stream of cash flows from timetto timeT—for example, a bond that makes periodic coupon payments. Theexpected returnsof such claims over the horizonHcannot be computed without knowing thereinvestment strategythat invests the cash flows maturingbeforetimeH. Like the case in the second point above, all of the cash flows maturing before timeHcan be reinvested in another security—for example, anH-maturity pure discount bond or a money market account—for the remaining duration until timeH, and the expected future value of these reinvested cash flows can be computed numerically using theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure.
Fourth, though this paper focuses on the derivation of analytical solutions for expected returns of contingent claims, future research can use the EEM theory to derive analytical solutions of the variance, covariance, and other higher-order moments of contingent claim returns.31Also, various numerical methods, such as trees, finite difference methods, and Monte Carlo methods, can be used to obtain higher-order moments of the return on a portfolio of claims under theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure. Furthermore, various finite-horizon tail risk measures (such as value-at-risk, conditional VaR, expected shortfall, and others) for contingent claim portfolio returns can be obtained with Monte Carlo simulations under theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure. Thus, EEMs such as theR${\mathbb {R}}$measure can significantly advance the risk and return analysis of fixed-income and financial derivative portfolios.
Finally, perhaps, the most important empirical application of the analytical solutions of the expected future prices of contingent claims obtained using the EEM theory is that various methods such as Markov chain Monte Carlo and generalized method of moments (see Hansen (1982)) become feasible for studying both thecross-sectionand theterm structureof returns of a variety of contingent claims, such as Treasury bonds, corporate bonds, interest rate derivatives, credit derivatives, equity derivatives, and others. It is our hope that the EEM theory can shift the focus of research from mostly valuation tobothvaluation and expected returns, for a variety of finite-maturity securities and claims used in the fixed-income markets and the derivatives markets.
Before concluding, we would like to underscore an important point regarding the derivations of the expected future prices of different contingent claims under various classes of models in the equity, interest rate, and credit risk areas, given in SectionsIIandIII, and inInternet AppendixSectionsII,III,IV,V, andVI. Since the EEMs and theR-transforms provide asingle analytical solutionfor the expected future price (which includes both the current price forH=t$H = t$and the expected future price forH>t$H > t$), and since a given EEM and a givenR-transform always nest the associated EMM and the associatedQ-transform, respectively, for the special case in whichH=t$H=t$, all future work on the derivations of the analytical solutions of the expected future prices of contingent claims can use either the EEMs or theR-transforms instead of the associated EMMs or the associatedQ-transforms, respectively. Since the expected future price is the most relevant input, and in many cases, the only input required for computing the expected return of a contingent claim, use of the EEMs or theR-transforms kills two birds with one stone—it allows for the derivation of a single analytical solution used in bothvaluationand computing theexpected returnof the claim.
